title: CDMGNotation

lean_statement.name: Membership
lean_statement.code:
instance : Membership α (CDMG α) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V

lean_statement.name: tuh
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN's `\tuh`: there is a *directed* edge `v₁ → v₂` in `G`, i.e.
`(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E`. -/
def tuh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E

/-- LN `v₁ ⟶[G] v₂`: directed edge `v₁ → v₂` in the CDMG `G`. Matches
LN macro `\tuh`. Unicode arrow is `⟶` (`\longrightarrow`, U+27F6),
chosen distinct from the function arrow `→` to avoid any clash with
Lean's built-in syntax. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟶[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.tuh G v₁ v₂

lean_statement.name: hut
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN's `\hut`: there is a *directed* edge `v₁ ← v₂` in `G`, i.e.
`(v₂, v₁) ∈ G.E` (the same `G.E` membership, with arguments swapped). -/
def hut (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₂, v₁) ∈ G.E

/-- LN `v₁ ⟵[G] v₂`: directed edge `v₁ ← v₂` in `G`. Matches LN macro
`\hut`. Equivalent to `v₂ ⟶[G] v₁` by definition. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟵[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hut G v₁ v₂

lean_statement.name: huh
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN's `\huh`: there is a *bidirected* edge `v₁ ↔ v₂` in `G`, i.e.
`(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L`. Because `G.L` is required to be symmetric (see
`CDMG.L_symm` in `def_3_1`), `huh G v₁ v₂` and `huh G v₂ v₁` are
propositionally equivalent. -/
def huh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L

/-- LN `v₁ ⟷[G] v₂`: bidirected edge `v₁ ↔ v₂` in `G`. Matches LN
macro `\huh`. Symmetric in `v₁`/`v₂` thanks to `CDMG.L_symm`. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.huh G v₁ v₂

lean_statement.name: suh
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN's `\suh`: there is an edge with an arrowhead at `v₂` in `G`,
i.e. either `tuh G v₁ v₂` (`→`) or `huh G v₁ v₂` (`↔`). -/
def suh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ⇸[G] v₂`: there is an arrowhead at `v₂` (`→` or `↔`).
Matches LN macro `\suh`. Unicode is `⇸` (U+21F8, RIGHTWARDS ARROW
WITH VERTICAL STROKE) -- the vertical stroke evokes the LN's star
placeholder at the left endpoint. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇸[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.suh G v₁ v₂

lean_statement.name: hus
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN's `\hus`: there is an edge with an arrowhead at `v₁` in `G`,
i.e. either `hut G v₁ v₂` (`←`) or `huh G v₁ v₂` (`↔`). -/
def hus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ⇷[G] v₂`: there is an arrowhead at `v₁` (`←` or `↔`).
Matches LN macro `\hus`. Unicode is `⇷` (U+21F7, LEFTWARDS ARROW
WITH VERTICAL STROKE). -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hus G v₁ v₂

lean_statement.name: sus
lean_statement.code:
/-- LN's `\sus`: there is *some* edge between `v₁` and `v₂` in `G`,
i.e. any of `tuh G v₁ v₂`, `hut G v₁ v₂`, or `huh G v₁ v₂`. This is
the "adjacency" relation used in def 3.3. -/
def sus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop :=
  tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ↮[G] v₂`: any edge of any orientation between `v₁` and
`v₂` ("adjacency"). Matches LN macro `\sus`. Unicode is `↮` (U+21AE,
LEFT RIGHT ARROW WITH STROKE) -- the doubled stroke evokes a
placeholder at both endpoints. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ↮[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.sus G v₁ v₂

lean_explanation:
The Lean file equips the `CDMG α` structure from def 3.1 with the seven notations of the LN's `\begin{Not}` block. Throughout, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type — matching the LN's silent assumption that the graph is over some carrier — and `G : CDMG α` is the graph whose edges we are talking about; `v₁ v₂ : α` are arbitrary vertices.

Item 1 is registered as a Lean `Membership` typeclass instance, so the literal Lean expression `v ∈ G` typechecks and unfolds to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` — `Set` union membership of `v` in the input nodes $J$ and the output nodes $V$ of the CDMG. Using the typeclass (rather than a bespoke predicate like `CDMG.mem`) is what makes the LN's literal `v ∈ G` write the way it reads.

Items 2–7 are each a `Prop`-valued one-line `def` paired with a `scoped notation`. `tuh G v₁ v₂` (LN: $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$) unfolds to set membership $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$; `hut G v₁ v₂` is the same with arguments swapped, encoding $v_1 \hut v_2$; `huh G v₁ v₂` (LN: $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$) is membership in $G.L$. The three 'star' relations `suh`, `hus`, `sus` (LN: $\suh$, $\hus$, $\sus$) are then defined as disjunctions of those three primitives, mirroring the LN's reading of the star as 'arrowhead or tail': `suh = tuh ∨ huh`, `hus = hut ∨ huh`, `sus = tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`.

The accompanying `scoped notation` declarations install the visible arrows `⟶[G]`, `⟵[G]`, `⟷[G]`, `⇸[G]`, `⇷[G]`, `↮[G]`, each at parser precedence 50. `scoped` means they are only in scope after a downstream file does `open scoped Causality.CDMG`, so files that do not open the namespace pay no notational cost.
design_choices:
**The `Membership` typeclass instance rather than a bespoke `CDMG.mem` predicate.** The LN consistently writes $v \in G$, so we register `v ∈ G` as a Lean `Membership` typeclass instance and the LN's literal phrase typechecks unchanged. A custom predicate `CDMG.mem G v` would have forced that explicit spelling at every callsite and would not interact with Mathlib's library of `mem_*` lemmas (`Set.mem_union`, `Set.mem_insert_iff`, …); with the typeclass instance and its accompanying `simp` defining equation, the full Mathlib `Set`-membership toolkit applies automatically.

**Six separate `def`s, not one inductive `EdgeKind` enum.** An alternative was a single inductive type `EdgeKind` with constructors for directed / reverse-directed / bidirected edges and one predicate `hasEdge : EdgeKind → CDMG α → α → α → Prop`. We rejected this for two reasons. First, the LN treats $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ and its kin as *atoms* in proofs — for example, parents in def 3.5 are defined as $\{w \mid w \tuh v \in G\}$, and atoms compose better with `simp`, `rw`, and pattern matching than predicates wrapped around an enum constructor. Second, the three star relations are not single edge kinds but disjunctions of the primitive three; an enum would not represent them naturally, and we would end up writing the disjunctions anyway. Spelling them out directly mirrors the LN's own approach.

**`Prop`-valued, not `Bool`-valued.** Edges are propositions. This matches the LN, lets us write $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$ directly (since `G.E : Set (α × α)` is already `Prop`-valued), and avoids imposing `DecidableEq α` at this layer — later chapters of the lecture notes use real-valued (uncountable) vertex types, where decidable equality is unavailable. Where decidability is genuinely needed it will be introduced as a separate hypothesis.

**Unicode arrows distinct from Lean's overloaded short arrows.** For the three primitive edges we use the long arrows `⟶ ⟵ ⟷` (U+27F6/F5/F7) rather than the short `→ ← ↔`, which in Lean are already heavily overloaded — function arrows, `Iff`, rewrite direction, and more. For the star relations we use `⇸` and `⇷` (arrows with a vertical stroke), where the stroke visually echoes the LN's star placeholder at the corresponding endpoint, and `↮` (left-right arrow with stroke) for the symmetric 'any edge' relation. Carrying the graph argument as a bracketed infix `[G]` keeps the notation unambiguous and sidesteps the awkwardness of trying to overload Lean's `∈`.

**`scoped`, not global notation.** All arrow notations live under the `Causality.CDMG` namespace and only activate after `open scoped Causality.CDMG`. A file that does not open the namespace pays no notational cost — useful given how many Unicode tokens we introduce in one place.
